name: Chinese Food: Street Food & Breakfast
whatsNew: 8 street-food and breakfast favourites for the Chinese Food section — jianbing, baozi, congee, youtiao, scallion pancakes, roujiamo, lamb skewers and tanghulu.

recipes.name: Jianbing
recipes.description: China's favourite breakfast on the go — a thin savoury crepe cooked on a griddle, cracked with an egg, brushed with sauces and folded around a crisp cracker.
ingredients: Batter: 1/2 cup flour, 1/4 cup mung bean flour, 1 cup water
1 crisp fried cracker (baocui) or a handful of crisps
steps: Spread a thin layer of batter on a hot, lightly oiled flat pan or crepe maker.
Crack an egg on top and spread it, then scatter scallions and coriander.
Flip, brush with hoisin and chili sauce, add the crispy cracker and pickles.
Fold into a parcel and serve hot.

recipes.name: Steamed Pork Buns
recipes.description: Soft, pillowy steamed buns with a savoury pork filling — a breakfast-stall staple eaten warm from the basket.
ingredients: 2 tbsp soy, 1 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tsp sesame oil
steps: Mix the pork with the seasonings into a sticky filling.
Roll the proved dough into discs, thicker in the centre.
Add filling and pleat the edges up into a sealed twist.
Prove until puffy, then steam for 12-15 minutes; rest before opening the lid.

recipes.name: Congee
recipes.description: Silky rice porridge simmered until the grains collapse — gentle comfort food, dressed up with savoury toppings.
ingredients: Toppings: century egg, shredded pork, scallions
Ginger, white pepper, soy to taste
steps: Rinse the rice, then simmer with the water and ginger on low heat.
Stir occasionally for about 50 minutes until thick and creamy.
Season with salt, soy and white pepper.
Ladle into bowls and add your toppings.

recipes.name: Fried Dough Sticks
recipes.description: Long, airy, golden crullers — torn and dunked in warm soy milk, or tucked inside a jianbing. The classic Chinese breakfast pairing.
ingredients: 1 tsp baking powder + 1/2 tsp baking soda + 1/2 tsp salt
steps: Mix a soft dough, then rest it (ideally overnight) for an airy crumb.
Cut into strips, press two together with a chopstick down the middle.
Stretch and slide into hot oil (190°C), turning until puffed and golden.
Drain and serve immediately with warm soy milk.

recipes.name: Scallion Pancakes
recipes.description: Flaky, chewy, pan-fried flatbreads layered with scallions and oil — shatteringly crisp outside, soft within.
ingredients: 2 tbsp oil + 1 tsp salt for the layers
steps: Make a soft dough with hot water and rest 30 minutes.
Roll thin, brush with oil, salt and scallions, then roll into a rope and coil.
Flatten the coil and roll out again to trap the layers.
Pan-fry in oil until golden and crisp on both sides; cut into wedges.

recipes.name: Roujiamo
recipes.description: Often called the world's oldest hamburger — a crisp griddled flatbread stuffed with richly braised, finely chopped pork. A Xi'an street legend.
ingredients: Braise: soy, Shaoxing wine, star anise, cinnamon, ginger, sugar
Mo (flatbread) dough, or split round buns
steps: Braise the pork low and slow until meltingly tender, then chop finely.
Griddle the flatbreads until crisp and slightly puffed.
Split each bread open into a pocket.
Stuff with the chopped pork, a spoon of braising liquid and coriander.

recipes.name: Cumin Lamb Skewers
recipes.description: The smoky, cumin-dusted skewers that sizzle on every Chinese night-market grill — a Xinjiang favourite gone nationwide.
ingredients: 2 tbsp ground cumin, 1 tbsp chili flakes
steps: Thread the lamb onto skewers, alternating lean and fat.
Grill over charcoal or a hot griddle, turning often.
Dust generously with cumin, chili and salt as they cook.
Serve hot off the grill, ideally with cold beer.

recipes.name: Tanghulu
recipes.description: Skewers of tart hawthorn berries dipped in a glassy sugar shell that cracks with a satisfying snap — a winter-street treat.
steps: Thread the fruit onto bamboo skewers and wipe them dry.
Boil the sugar and water to hard-crack stage (~150°C) without stirring.
Quickly roll each skewer in the hot syrup to coat thinly.
Set on an oiled tray until the shell hardens, then eat fresh.
